1: The invitation letters to ‘all Israel and Judah’ and specifically to ‘Ephraim and Manasseh’ — written after the 722–720 BCE Assyrian conquest of the Northern Kingdom (2 Kgs 17) — address an audience that no longer exists as an independent political entity. The northern tribes are under Assyrian provincial administration; the invitation is addressed to the surviving remnant population that was not deported. Hezekiah’s reach into Assyrian-controlled territory for a religious invitation reflects the Chronicler’s pan-Israelite vision: political division does not dissolve covenantal unity. The mention of Ephraim and Manasseh by name (the two largest and most central northern tribes) is strategic outreach.
2: The legal basis for a second-month Passover: Num 9:6-14 explicitly provides for individuals unable to celebrate the Passover in the first month (due to corpse defilement or being on a distant journey) to celebrate it exactly one month later — the fourteenth of the second month. Hezekiah’s innovation is to apply this individual provision at a national level. The legal reasoning requires consensus: wayyiwwāʿaṣ hammelek wᵉśārāyw wᵉkol-haqqāhāl (the king consulted with his officials and the whole congregation) — this is a royal-assembly decision, not a unilateral royal action.
3: The two reasons given for the second-month choice: (1) insufficient consecrated priests (lōʾ-yāḵᵉlû lāʿăśōtāh bāʿēt hahîʾ kî hakᵉhānîm lōʾ-hiqqaddᵉšû lᵉdî) and (2) the people had not assembled in Jerusalem (wᵉhāʿām lōʾ-ʾāsᵃpû). The priestly consecration backlog is confirmed by v34 of ch29. The assembly problem reflects the logistics of gathering a dispersed population including northerners — impossible to achieve before the fourteenth of the first month given the short preparation window after the temple purification (completed on the sixteenth of the first month, v17).
4: ‘The plan seemed right to the king and the whole assembly’ (wayyiṭᵉšar haddāḇār bᵉʿênê hammelek ûḇᵉʿênê kol-haqqāhāl) — the consensus-approval formula marks the second-month Passover as the result of genuine corporate deliberation. The Chronicler’s emphasis on assembly (qāhāl) decision-making throughout Hezekiah’s reform demonstrates a model of legitimate royal-communal governance: even the king’s religious initiatives require assembly ratification.
5: ‘From Beersheba to Dan’ (miBᵉʾēr-šāḇaʿ wᵉʿad-Dān) — the standard phrase for the geographic extent of all Israel (cf. Judg 20:1; 2 Sam 3:10), here applied to the Passover summons. Beersheba was at the southern limit of Judah; Dan at the northern limit of the former Northern Kingdom, now under Assyrian control. The geographic formula asserts the Israelite unity claim that underlies the entire Passover initiative. The note that the Passover lōʾ-ʿāśūhû lāhōḇ had not been kept as prescribed (in great numbers) acknowledges the historical irregularity of national Passover observance.
6: The royal couriers (hārāṣîm: runners, express messengers) carried official letters (ʾiggᵉrôt) throughout Israel and Judah. The call to ‘return to the LORD, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel’ — using the patriarchal names (ʾĂḇrāhām Yiṣḥāq wᵉYiśrāʾēl) rather than the usual ‘God of your fathers’ — deliberately invokes the covenant ancestors as the foundation for the return invitation. The promise to the northern remnant (šᵉʾērît hannimleṭet lākem, survivors who have escaped): even the Assyrian captives might benefit if those who remain return to YHWH.
7: The negative example of ‘your fathers and brothers who were unfaithful’ (mᵉʿᵃlû ḇaYHWH) explicitly references the fate of the Northern Kingdom. wayyittᵉnēm lᵉšammāh (‘he made them a horror’) — the same judgment-term (šammāh) used in 29:8 for Judah’s own condition. The Chronicler uses the Northern Kingdom’s fate as a cautionary lesson for both the northern remnant and the southern kingdom: the pattern of unfaithfulness → desolation is well-established and repeatable.
8: ‘Do not be stiff-necked like your ancestors’ (ʾal-taqšû ʿarᵉpᵉḵem) — qāšāh ʿōrep (stiff neck/hard neck) is the standard Pentateuchal description of Israelite stubbornness before YHWH (Exod 32:9; 33:3, 5; 34:9; Deut 9:6, 13). The letter cites the Mosaic tradition explicitly as the ground for its appeal. lāḇôʾ lᵉmiqdāšô ʾăšer hiqdîš lᵉʿôlām (‘come to his sanctuary, which he has consecrated forever’) — the temple’s eternal consecration is the basis for its continued validity as a worship center even after the Assyrian crisis.
9: The promise of compassion from captors (yᵉraḥᵃmûkem ᵃšîḇêhem) for those in Assyrian exile is the only explicit hope expressed in the letter for the deported northerners. The logic: if the northern remnant returns to YHWH, their deported relatives (ʾaḥêḵem ûḇᵉnêḵem) will find compassion from their Assyrian captors and return. This reflects ANE diplomatic practice where a conquered people’s religious reform could affect their political status with the conqueror. The theological ground: ḥannûn wᵉraḥûm YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵem (the LORD your God is gracious and merciful) — the Mosaic creed of divine character (Exod 34:6) becomes the pastoral basis for the appeal.
10: The mockery by the northerners — wayyihyû mᵉśaḥᵃqîm ʿălêhem ûmalbizîm (they laughed and mocked at them) — reflects the social-political reality of the northern population under Assyrian governance. The remnant communities that remained after the 722 BCE deportation had adapted to the new political and religious reality, including the syncretic worship described in 2 Kgs 17:24-41 (the settlers from Babylon, Cuthah, and other regions brought by Assyria, mixing their own cults with vestigial YHWH-worship). Hezekiah’s purely Yahwistic invitation would have seemed politically dangerous and religiously archaic to them.
11: The positive response from some in three northern tribes: Asher, Manasseh, and Zebulun. These three tribes are geographically distributed across the former Northern Kingdom’s territory (Asher = far northwest/Galilee coast; Manasseh = central highlands; Zebulun = lower Galilee). Their response is characterized as kāniʿû (they humbled themselves) — the same verb used for proper response to prophetic rebuke (cf. 12:6-7; 30:6, 8; 32:26; 33:12). Humbling oneself before YHWH is the prerequisite for all genuine cultic participation in Chronicles.
12: ‘God’s hand gave them one heart’ (ûḇîhûdāh hāyᵉtāh yad hāʾĕlōhîm lātēt lāhem lēḇ ʾeḥāḏ) — the divine unification of Judah’s heart to carry out the reform is the Chronicler’s standard explanation for human unanimity in religious matters. lēḇ ʾeḥāḏ (one heart) echoes the promise of Jer 32:39 (‘I will give them one heart and one way’) and Ezek 11:19 (‘I will give them an undivided heart’). The unanimity is not achieved by political pressure but by divine action.
13: The ‘Festival of Unleavened Bread’ (ḥag hammaṣṣôt) in the second month — the seven-day festival following Passover (Lev 23:6-8; Num 28:17-25) is celebrated here as the context for the great assembly. The two festivals (Passover = 14th of the month; Unleavened Bread = 15th-21st) are distinct in Mosaic law but closely connected in practice. The assembly (qāhāl rab mᵉʾōd, a very great assembly) represents the largest gathering since Solomon’s dedication — the comparison in v26 will make this explicit.
14: The spontaneous iconoclasm — removing altars (hammizᵉḇᵉḥôt) and incense altars (hammiqṭārôt) from Jerusalem and throwing them into the Kidron Brook — precedes the formal Passover celebration. The worshippers’ own initiative in purifying the city before the feast reflects the Passover requirement of removing leaven (Exod 12:15) extended to all forms of ritual impurity. The Kidron as the dump for cultic impurities (cf. 29:16) is used again; the brook functions as the ritual boundary between the sacred city and the profane world beyond.
15: The slaughter of the Passover lamb on the fourteenth day of the second month precisely fulfills the Num 9:6-14 provision — the lamb killed on the fourteenth, the festival lasting the fifteenth through twenty-first. The priests and Levites’ shame (wayyēḇōšû) at their insufficient consecration level — compared to the laity’s enthusiasm — is the same theme as 29:34: the institutional personnel lag behind the popular religious momentum. Their response: consecrating themselves and bringing burnt offerings into the LORD’s house as an act of self-correction.
16: ‘They took their positions as prescribed, following the law of Moses the man of God’ (wayyaʿᵃmᵉdû ʿal-ʿomᵉdām kᵉmišpāṭām kᵉtôrat Mōšeh ʾîš-hāʾĕlōhîm) — the Mosaic prescriptions govern both position and procedure; each cult official stands at their assigned station. The Levites received blood (haldūyîm lᵉqabbēl) and the priests sprinkled it — a procedural adaptation from the normal Passover practice (Exod 12:21-27, where each family slaughters its own lamb) to a centralized temple liturgy. The Chronicler is aware that the original Passover was a household observance; the centralized temple version is an adaptation for national assembly.
17: The Levites slaughtering the Passover lambs for the ritually unclean is a significant extension of the Passover provisions. Normally each family slaughtered its own animal (Exod 12:3-6); here the Levites act as sacrificial proxies for the ritually impure. This reflects a pastoral-theological decision: better to allow the unclean to participate through Levitical mediation than to exclude them from the national covenant feast. The Levites’ role expands here beyond what the Mosaic texts specify, demonstrating the flexibility of the Chronicler’s approach to ritual within the framework of seeking YHWH with a right heart.
18: ‘A large number of people from Ephraim, Manasseh, Issachar, and Zebulun had not purified themselves, yet they ate the Passover contrary to what was written’ — four northern tribes are explicitly named as ritually non-compliant participants. The Chronicler does not condemn them for this but records Hezekiah’s intercession. The phrase kî ʾāḵᵉlû min-happesaḥ bᵉlōʾ kakkātûḇ (‘they ate the Passover contrary to what was written’) is a frank acknowledgment of ritual non-compliance; the Chronicler’s pastoral resolution (vv18b-20) is that sincere heart + seeking God = valid worship despite ritual irregularity.
19: Hezekiah’s intercessory prayer for the ritually impure: kol-lēḇāḇô hēḵîn liḏrōš ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm YHWH (‘everyone who has set his heart to seek God, the LORD, the God of his ancestors’) — the criterion for valid participation is heart-intention (hēḵîn lēḇ = prepared/set the heart), not ritual compliance. The phrase wᵉlōʾ kᵉṭohrat haqqōḏeš (‘even if not cleansed according to the sanctuary’s rules’) explicitly names the ritual deficit while subordinating it to the motivational criterion. This is one of the Hebrew Bible’s clearest pastoral statements that sincere seeking of YHWH can compensate for involuntary ritual non-compliance.
20: wayyišmaʿ YHWH ʾel-Yᵉḥizqiyyāhû wayyirpāʾ ʾet-hāʿām (‘the LORD listened to Hezekiah and healed the people’) — the healing (rāpāʾ) in response to intercessory prayer is the direct answer to the ritual irregularity. The verb rāpāʾ can mean both physical healing and the removal of guilt/impurity; here it most naturally means the ritual/moral healing of the impurity that would have made their Passover participation invalid. YHWH’s acceptance of the impure participants through Hezekiah’s intercession is the structural parallel to the High Priest’s annual atonement covering Israel’s inadvertent sins (Lev 16:16).
21: Seven days of celebration with great joy (bᵉśimḥāh gᵉdôlāh) — the Levites and priests praised the LORD daily with kᵉlê-ʿōz lYHWH (resounding instruments for YHWH; ʿōz = strength/loud; instruments of power). The term kᵉlê-ʿōz appears only here and Ps 150:4; the expression captures the volume and intensity of the temple musical performance. The daily praise throughout the seven-day feast represents a sustained liturgical performance unprecedented in Hezekiah’s reign and perhaps since Solomon’s dedication.
22: Hezekiah spoke ʿal-lēḇ kol-halᵉwiyyim haMaskîlîm śēḵel ṭôḇ laYHWH (‘encouragingly to all the Levites who showed skill and understanding in the LORD’s service’) — maśkîl śēḵel ṭôḇ: Levites with good skill/insight in YHWH’s service. The maśkîl (one who has skill or insight) appears as a psalm superscription title (Pss 32, 42, 44, 45 et al.) connected to skilled musical-didactic composition. Fellowship offerings (šᵉlāmîm) throughout seven days plus confession of praise (mîtᵉwaddîm; from yādāh in Hithpael = giving thanks/confessing) combine sacrificial and verbal worship.
23: The unanimous decision to extend the festival another seven days (lāʿăśôt yāmîm šiḇᵃʿāh ʾăḥērîm) — a spontaneous popular extension of the festival beyond the prescribed seven days. This extension has no Mosaic precedent; it is an act of pure devotional enthusiasm. Only Solomon’s dedication (2 Chr 7:8-10) provides a partial parallel: Solomon’s feast ran eight days (seven for the Feast of Booths + one day of assembly). Hezekiah’s fourteen total days (7+7) thus exceeds any previous festival duration, marking his Passover as uniquely joyful in Israel’s history.
24: The quantitative scale of royal and official contributions: Hezekiah gave 1,000 bulls and 7,000 sheep for the congregation; the officials gave 1,000 bulls and 10,000 sheep. The total — 2,000 bulls and 17,000 sheep — is enormous but less than Solomon’s dedication (22,000 oxen + 120,000 sheep; 2 Chr 7:5). The priestly consecrations during the extended festival (wᵉkōhᵃnîm harbēh hiqqaddāšû) echoes 29:34: the priestly preparation that was insufficient for the initial reform catches up during the extended feast.
25: The full enumeration of who rejoiced: the whole congregation of Judah (kol-qᵉhal Yᵉhûdāh), the priests, the Levites, all who came from Israel (haqqāhāl habbāʾ mêYiśrāʾēl), the foreigners who came from Israel (wᵉhagārîm haggārîm meʾereṣ Yiśrāʾēl), and those living in Judah. The gārîm (resident aliens) participating in the Passover is explicitly grounded in the Mosaic law (Exod 12:48-49; Num 9:14), which permits circumcised resident aliens to celebrate. The pan-Israelite and even multi-ethnic scope of the celebration is the Chronicler’s fullest expression of inclusive covenant community.
26: ‘There was great joy in Jerusalem — nothing like it since the time of Solomon son of David king of Israel’ — the explicit comparison to Solomon’s dedication festivals positions Hezekiah’s Passover as the single most significant liturgical event in the entire post-Solomonic period. The Chronicler’s comparison is also a theological claim: Hezekiah’s reform has restored Judah to the quality of covenant life that prevailed under the united monarchy. This comparison recurs in the Josiah narrative (35:18: ‘no Passover like it had been kept in Israel since the days of Samuel the prophet’) — both reformers restore what was lost.
27: The priests and Levites’ blessing (yᵉḇārᵉḵû ʾet-hāʿām) is the formal Aaronic blessing of Num 6:22-27, the prescribed priestly act for dismissing the congregation after worship. wayyiqqaḇēl qôlām (‘their voice was heard’) — the passive is significant: the priests’ voice was received/accepted by God, not merely heard by the congregation. wayyāḇōʾ tᵉpillātām lᵉmᵉʿôn qoḏšô laššāmāyim (‘their prayer ascended to his holy dwelling place — to heaven’) — the ascending prayer formula at the Passover’s conclusion (cf. Solomon’s prayer reaching heaven in 2 Chr 6:21, 23, 25). The feast ends with the confirmation that the prayers have reached the divine throne.
